positions.claim: AI will function primarily as a complement to labor rather than a substitute, preventing mass unemployment
implicitAssumptions: The task composition changes from AI will create new labor demand categories faster than they eliminate existing ones
Complementarity effects dominate substitution effects at current and near-term AI capability levels
Historical patterns of technological revolution (where labor wasn't eliminated) will hold for AI despite its different characteristics

positions.claim: Current labor market data (e.g., software engineer postings up 11% YoY) demonstrates that AI displacement fears are empirically unfounded
implicitAssumptions: Current job posting data is a leading indicator of AI's labor market impact rather than a lagging indicator
The 11% YoY growth represents a steady-state signal rather than a temporary lag before displacement accelerates
Software engineering is a representative canary sector for AI displacement rather than an exceptional case

positions.claim: AI-driven productivity shocks will expand potential output and real incomes without reducing aggregate labor demand
implicitAssumptions: Productivity gains will translate into demand expansion for existing goods/services rather than pure price deflation
The composition of demand will shift toward labor-intensive sectors as productivity frees up purchasing power
Income effects from productivity gains will outweigh substitution effects on labor demand

positions.claim: Compute costs will rise to meet or exceed human labor costs, creating a natural economic brake on labor substitution
implicitAssumptions: Compute demand growth from automation will outpace Moore's Law and manufacturing capacity expansion
Compute costs are primarily constrained by physical production capacity rather than algorithmic efficiency improvements
The marginal cost curve for compute is upward-sloping in the relevant time horizon rather than continuing to decline exponentially

positions.claim: Technology adoption growth rates are self-limiting through declining marginal adopter productivity and infrastructure constraints
implicitAssumptions: AI adoption follows a standard diffusion curve where early adopters capture disproportionate gains
Complementary infrastructure (data pipelines, organizational redesign, training) develops slower than core AI capabilities
The productivity distribution across potential adopters is wide enough that marginal adopters significantly drag down aggregate adoption rates

positions.claim: Extreme labor displacement scenarios require implausibly high elasticity of substitution between AI capital and human labor
implicitAssumptions: Current economic production functions have fundamental complementarities that AI cannot overcome
The elasticity of substitution is a stable parameter rather than something that could shift dramatically with capability breakthroughs
Labor's share of income collapsing would require near-perfect substitutability, which is empirically implausible given current AI limitations

positions.claim: Even if labor displacement occurred, aggregate demand would not automatically collapse because redistribution mechanisms would activate
implicitAssumptions: Political systems will implement redistribution before aggregate demand failures become severe
The threshold for 'persistent failure of redistribution mechanisms' is high enough that it represents a tail risk rather than a central scenario
Investment opportunities would remain available to absorb capital income even in high-automation scenarios

positions.claim: Productivity gains will lead to workweek reduction rather than unemployment
implicitAssumptions: Labor markets will adjust through hours reduction rather than headcount reduction
Workers have sufficient bargaining power or institutional support to capture productivity gains as leisure rather than being displaced
The preference for leisure over income is strong enough at current income levels to drive workweek reduction

positions.claim: Current AI displacement narratives reflect premature certainty about unknowable future outcomes
implicitAssumptions: The burden of proof lies with displacement proponents rather than with those claiming continuity
Absence of current evidence of displacement is strong evidence against future displacement rather than merely uninformative
Extrapolating from current trends is more epistemically sound than modeling potential discontinuities

positions.claim: Marginal cost reductions from productivity shocks do not automatically translate to labor displacement
implicitAssumptions: Lower marginal costs primarily drive output expansion rather than input reduction
Demand elasticity for AI-enhanced goods/services is high enough to absorb productivity gains through volume growth
Firms optimize for growth rather than pure cost minimization when productivity shocks occur

positions.claim: The capital income vs. wage income consumption differential is insufficient to cause demand collapse in realistic scenarios
implicitAssumptions: The marginal propensity to consume gap between capital and labor income is modest enough that redistribution mechanisms can compensate
Capital income recipients will maintain sufficient consumption or investment to prevent demand spirals
The scenario where capital income dominates AND has low MPC AND redistribution fails represents a conjunction of unlikely events

positions.claim: Technological revolutions alter task composition without eliminating labor in aggregate
implicitAssumptions: The historical pattern of task recomposition creating new labor demand categories will continue with AI
Human comparative advantage is robust enough that new tasks will emerge even as AI capabilities expand
The speed of task elimination will not exceed the speed of new task creation by enough to cause persistent unemployment

representativeClaims: productivity growth → workweek reduction
productivity gains → composition of demand alteration
productivity gains → real incomes expansion
productivity gains → new industries generation
productivity gains ⊣ labor withdrawal
AI-driven automation → productivity shock
productivity shocks ⊣ marginal costs
productivity shocks → potential output
productivity shocks → real income
major technological advances (steam power, electrification, internal combustion engine, computing) → productivity shock pattern
technological revolutions → task composition alteration
AI as complement to labor ⊣ labor demand reduction
technological revolutions ⊣ labor elimination
Microsoft Office advent → office worker labor demand
near-total automation of economically relevant labor → negative demand shock large enough to overwhelm output expansion
automation expansion → compute demand
increased compute demand → marginal cost of compute
marginal cost of compute rises above marginal cost of human labor ⊣ substitution of human labor by automation
constraints (physical capital, energy availability, regulatory approvals, organizational change) ⊣ economic deployment of automation
natural economic boundary to automation → lack of substitution occurring
costs fall → growth acceleration in technology adoption
marginal adopter productivity/profitability decline → growth deceleration
complementary infrastructure development → growth acceleration in technology adoption
saturation in technology adoption → growth deceleration
extremely high elasticity of substitution between AI capital and labor → labor's share of income collapses
persistent failure of redistribution mechanisms and simultaneous drying up of investment opportunities → structural fall in aggregate demand
capital income having lower marginal propensity to consume than wage income ⊣ aggregate demand automatically imploding
rising software engineer job postings (up 11% YoY) → undermines displacement narrative
current displacement narrative about AI ⊣ certainty about future labor destruction
